mnemonics.title: Process States - NEW READY RUNNING WAITING TERMINATED
mnemonics.content: NEW READY RUNNING WAITING TERMINATED - NEVER READY TO WORK, JUST WAIT! (N-R-R-W-T)

mnemonics.title: PCB Contents
mnemonics.content: PID, STATE, PC, REGISTERS, MEMORY INFO, I/O STATUS, ACCOUNTING - 'PROPERLY STORING MY IMPORTANT ACCOUNT DOCUMENTS' (P=Process info, S=State, M=Memory, I=I/O, A=Accounting, D=Data)

mnemonics.title: CPU Scheduling Criteria
mnemonics.content: CPU UTILIZATION, THROUGHPUT, TURNAROUND, WAITING, RESPONSE - 'UTTARWADI' (UTilization, Throughput, Turnaround, Waiting, Response, And so on)

mnemonics.title: Types of Scheduling Algorithms
mnemonics.content: PREEMPTIVE vs NON-PREEMPTIVE - 'PREEMPTIVE: PRE (before) - you can stop before finishing. NON: you finish the job!'

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing all process information
Scheduling algorithm minimizing average wait time

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with 5 states: NEW→READY arrow, READY→RUNNING arrow (scheduler), RUNNING→READY arrow (preemption), RUNNING→WAITING arrow (I/O wait), WAITING→READY arrow (I/O complete), RUNNING→TERMINATED arrow. This creates a cycle showing state transitions.

Imagine PCB as a folder with tabs: PROCESS INFO TAB (PID, parent PID), CPU STATE TAB (PC, registers, flags), MEMORY MANAGEMENT TAB (page tables, limits), ACCOUNTING TAB (CPU time used, limits), I/O STATUS TAB (open files, devices).
Draw a rectangle as PROCESS: inside show 3 smaller rectangles as THREADS. Threads share the middle 'shared resources' box (code, data, heap) but each has own 'private' box (PC, registers, stack).
Think of CPU as a desk. Context switch = clearing desk (save current work to folder/PCB), then getting new folder (load new process). The more processes, more folder swapping = more overhead.

analogies.analogy: A process is like a factory order. It has a work order (code), current work status (PC, registers), workspace (memory), and production record (PCB). Multiple factories (processes) can run independently.
NEW = order received, not started. READY = on workbench waiting. RUNNING = actively being worked on. WAITING = paused waiting for materials. TERMINATED = order completed.
The CPU scheduler is like a manager in a kitchen. Based on strategy (FCFS, SJF, etc.), decides which chef (process) gets the stove (CPU) next.
Semaphore is like a bathroom key - only one person can enter at a time. If key is taken (count=0), others must wait. After use, key is returned (count=1).
fork() is like cell division - parent cell (process) splits into two identical cells. Both have same 'genetic information' (code, data) but are separate living entities with own identity (PIDs).
